channel.title: State-Policy Think-Tank Digest
channel.description: Latest posts from free-market policy institutes across the U.S.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Maine Taxpayers Are Once Again Paying for Bellows’ Fight with Trump
item.description:
After a controversial Bureau of Motor Vehicles decision, the Maine taxpayers are once again being dragged into a high-profile constitutional fight with the Trump administration. The U.S. Department of Justice has sued the State of Maine, Governor Janet Mills, Attorney General Aaron Frey, and Secretary of State Shenna Bellows over a Bureau of Motor Vehicles […]

The post Maine Taxpayers Are Once Again Paying for Bellows’ Fight with Trump appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Beacontn] First Principles Series: Don’t Ban Data Centers. Don’t Subsidize Them, Either.
item.description: We can allow data centers to exist while keeping the courts open for nuisance lawsuits against bad actors who infringe upon protections like water rights when other property owners are actually harmed by them.

item.title: [Rifreedom] Un-Affordability: Speaker Blazejewski’s Budget Denies Science, Will Raise Energy Costs for Ocean Staters … but Center Claims One Major Victory
item.description:
Despite common-sense energy reforms suggested by Governor McKee; despite pledged reforms by all other New England states; and despite indisputable research that shows how Rhode Island’s net zero emissions energy strategy will lead to unaffordable costs and dangerous risks … the budget put forth by the new Speaker of the RI House of Representative, Christopher Blazejewski … doubles-down on the state’s radical Act On Climate mandates.

The post Un-Affordability: Speaker Blazejewski’s Budget Denies Science, Will Raise Energy Costs for Ocean Staters … but Center Claims One Major Victory appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Property Tax Relief Options Explained
item.description: The most important point to understand regarding property taxes is that they are budget-driven. This simply means local taxing districts, which include counties, cities, schools, fire districts, etc., set property tax budgets, and those budgets are the primary driver of property tax payments and the changes (usually increases) over time. Long-term property tax relief requires […]

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] Circuit Court – Adams County
item.description:
Circuit Court – Adams County

The post Circuit Court – Adams County appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Coverage vs. Care (Part 1)
item.description: On Valentine’s Day 1966, Gov. Bob Smylie called a special session of the state Legislature to deal, primarily, with reapportioning the Idaho Senate to comply with the U.S. Supreme Court’s one-man-one-vote requirement. Back then, there was a senator for each of the state’s 44 counties, and the courts had ruled the arrangement unconstitutional. So when […]

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells’ $368,885 Secret Meeting
item.description:
For Immediate Release: June 10, 2026 Contact: press@reforminggovernment.org   IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells $368,885 Secret Meeting Lawsuit argues DPI violated open meetings laws, seeks to invalidate DPI’s secret meeting that weakened Wisconsin testing standards Delafield, Wis. — The Institute for Reforming Government (IRG), represented by the Wisconsin Transparency Project, is taking the Department […]

The post IRG Sues DPI Over Wisconsin Dells’ $368,885 Secret Meeting appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Reforminggovernment] Jake Curtis | Point Taken
item.description: The post Jake Curtis | Point Taken appeared first on Institute for Reforming Government.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Maine’s 2026 Primary Shows Why RCV Should Be Repealed
item.description:
Maine voters went to the polls on June 9, but in several of the state’s most important primary races, Election Day did not actually produce a winner. Instead, both gubernatorial primaries and the Democratic primary in Maine’s 2nd Congressional District are headed into ranked-choice voting tabulation. As a result, Mainers are left waiting days after […]

The post Maine’s 2026 Primary Shows Why RCV Should Be Repealed appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] PPS’ Failed Equity Policy: No Evidence, No Results
item.description:
By Naomi InmanTestimony by John A. Charles, Jr. Portland Public Schools briefly revisited...

The post PPS’ Failed Equity Policy: No Evidence, No Results appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jbartlett] Does Jeff Bezos benefit from a NH Business Enterprise Tax cut?
item.description:
New Hampshire’s unique state tax structure is hard to understand, especially for people who don’t own a business.  More than a third (36%) of the state General Fund and Education Trust Fund revenue comes from business taxes.  The vast majority of that comes from the Business Profits Tax (BPT), but the lesser-known Business Enterprise Tax […]

The post Does Jeff Bezos benefit from a NH Business Enterprise Tax cut? appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] Opinion piece: Report on economic development incentives is incomplete
item.description:
The following appeared in the Santa Fe New Mexican on June 6, 2026. It appeared in other papers across the state. Recently the Legislative Finance Committee produced a report in which (to summarize the findings) they made the case that New Mexico “spends” over half-a-billion dollars on tax breaks that generated little economic return for […]

The post Opinion piece: Report on economic development incentives is incomplete appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Arizona voters to decide on measure curtailing taxpayer support for teachers’ unions
item.description:
The Arizona Senate today passed House Concurrent Resolution 2040, a Freedom Foundation-backed measure that would prohibit public school districts from using taxpayer resources to support union organizing activities.

The post Arizona voters to decide on measure curtailing taxpayer support for teachers’ unions appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Empowerms] Dr. Branden Henry: How People Change
item.description:
Dr. Branden Henry: How People Change For years, Empower Mississippi has focused on removing external barriers to opportunity. But what if some of the barriers holding people back aren’t external at all? In this episode, Grant sits down with licensed professional counselor Dr. Brandon Henry to explore the internal obstacles that often prevent people from…

The post Dr. Branden Henry: How People Change first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraskanomics: Mark McHargue on Nebraska’s Agriculture Industry
item.description:
Tune in to the latest episode of Nebraskanomics as we welcome President of the Nebraska Farm Bureau, Mark McHargue. During the episode Mark and Platte CEO Jim Vokal discuss the impact of agriculture in Nebraska, next steps on property tax reform, and what the future looks like for our state’s largest industry.  Listen now on...

The post Nebraskanomics: Mark McHargue on Nebraska’s Agriculture Industry appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Rural Hospitals Need Prosperity
item.description: Kansas now has more rural hospitals at immediate risk of closure than any other state, according to KAKE News. That is serious. Rural healthcare access matters. Families should not have to drive for hours to access emergency care. But if Kansas treats this only as a hospital funding problem, it will miss the real issue. […]

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF on the road/post mortem on ABQ City Council victory
item.description:
This post will be a  lot less policy oriented than normal. That’s because as the head of RGF, this week has been VERY busy. It started with a HUGE win at ABQ City Council on Monday night when on a 5-4 vote Council (again) rejected a major tax increase. The vote was 5-4 against raising […]

The post RGF on the road/post mortem on ABQ City Council victory appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Launches The Wisconsin Education Comeback: A Roadmap for Student Success
item.description:
WILL has launched “The Wisconsin Education Comeback: A Roadmap for Student Success” a series of reports to reform education in Wisconsin. The roadmap will aim to provide a comprehensive set of K-12 education reforms for the next Governor and legislature to tackle.

The post WILL Launches The Wisconsin Education Comeback: A Roadmap for Student Success appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Will Law] Wisconsin Supreme Court Sides with WILL in Significant Legal Victory to End DEI
item.description:
WILL secured a significant victory for true equality under the law at the Wisconsin Supreme Court. The Court ruled that the racially discriminatory “Minority Undergraduate Retention Program” was unconstitutional and that taxpayers have standing in court to challenge such programs.

The post Wisconsin Supreme Court Sides with WILL in Significant Legal Victory to End DEI appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Foropportunity] When a Love Story Meets the Tax Code
item.description:
Key Points If you ask many engaged couples what’s worrying them before the big day, you’ll probably hear about things like catering […]

The post When a Love Story Meets the Tax Code appeared first on For Opportunity.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Wisconsin should choose the right side of the income tax divide
item.description:
The choice Wisconsin leaders make about income tax policy will have important implications for the state’s future competitiveness and prosperity.

The post Wisconsin should choose the right side of the income tax divide appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] NORTHJERSEY.COM: NY moves ahead on education. NJ should not be left behind | Opinion
item.description:
By Wells Winegar, Executive Director of the New Jersey Policy Institute, and Ashley Smith, Managing Director of Jersey 1st […]

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] IP28’s Radical Attempt to Kill Oregon’s Way of Life
item.description:
By Vanessa Mayerwith Naomi Inman Hunting, fishing, farming, and ranching have always been...

The post IP28’s Radical Attempt to Kill Oregon’s Way of Life appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Jbartlett] NH fishing & hunting license fees are among New England’s highest
item.description:
At the request of Gov. Kelly Ayotte, the New Hampshire Fish & Game Department has paused a proposed increase in fishing and hunting license fees.  Department officials say the increases are needed to shore up the budget. The last fee increase took effect in 2016.  A decade’s worth of inflation has eroded the value of […]

The post NH fishing & hunting license fees are among New England’s highest appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraska Population Growth: Are We Winning the Race for Residents?
item.description:
When measuring a state’s economic vitality, the most honest metric isn’t always in a government budget report—it’s in a moving van. Demographics are the bedrock of our workforce, tax base, and future prosperity. If we want to evaluate how Nebraska is truly performing, we have to look at whether we are attracting new talent or...

The post Nebraska Population Growth: Are We Winning the Race for Residents? appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Mspolicy] The Federal Scholarship Tax Credit – What You Need to Know
item.description:
Let me introduce you to three letters you are going to hear a great deal about in the months ahead: SGO. If they mean nothing to you today, they soon will. SGO stands for Scholarship Granting Organization — and thanks to a change in federal law, Mississippi is about to see a whole network of […]

The post The Federal Scholarship Tax Credit – What You Need to Know appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Portland Study Reinforces What We Already Know: Inclusionary Zoning is Terrible Policy
item.description:
For years, Maine Policy Institute has warned that Portland’s inclusionary zoning policy would make the city’s housing shortage worse, not better. Last December, when Portland commissioned yet another study on the subject, MPI noted that the evidence was already clear: Inclusionary zoning acts like a tax on housing development, raises the cost of building, and […]

The post Portland Study Reinforces What We Already Know: Inclusionary Zoning is Terrible Policy appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] Before New Jersey Becomes the Next Climate Superfund Test Case, Let’s See What the Courts Say
item.description:
By Wells Winegar The debate surrounding New Jersey’s proposed Climate Superfund Act has largely centered on climate policy. Supporters argue […]

The post Before New Jersey Becomes the Next Climate Superfund Test Case, Let’s See What the Courts Say first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Will Law] Judge Blocks Milwaukee’s Food Truck Ordinance Following WILL’s Lawsuit
item.description:
A Milwaukee County Circuit Court Judge blocked enforcement of Milwaukee’s 10:00 p.m. food truck curfew while the case continues through litigation. The order follows a lawsuit filed by WILL on behalf of The Fatty Patty food truck and its owner, Abdallah Ismail.

The post Judge Blocks Milwaukee’s Food Truck Ordinance Following WILL’s Lawsuit appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Affordability issue #1: Property tax increases far exceed most Kansans’ change in personal income
item.description: The #1 affordability issue facing many Kansans is property tax increases that are literally taxing some people out of their homes and prompting some small businesses to close. The average taxable assessed home value jumped 40% over the last four years, resulting in a 31% average property tax increase. Meanwhile, real (inflation-adjusted) per capita personal […]

item.title: [Rifreedom] ONE SCORE, Twenty Failures: The High Cost of Rhode Island’s One-Party Rule
item.description:
Rhode Island's twenty year single-party rule has driven sharp decline. The new “One Score” report details twenty major policy failures in taxes, affordability, education, business climate & more. Read it now.

The post ONE SCORE, Twenty Failures: The High Cost of Rhode Island’s One-Party Rule appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] Governor Sherrill is Targeting Junk Fees, but Where Do Government Fees Stand?
item.description:
by Megan Whitman NJPI Junior Policy Fellow   Governor Sherrill recently signed an Executive Order targeting “junk fees,” fees that […]

The post Governor Sherrill is Targeting Junk Fees, but Where Do Government Fees Stand? first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] The Continued Growth of the Four-Day School Week in Missouri
item.description:
The Missouri Department of Elementary and Secondary Education (DESE) reports that 188 of 518 school districts will be operating on a four-day school week (4dsw) during the upcoming school year. […]

The post The Continued Growth of the Four-Day School Week in Missouri appeared first on Show-Me Institute.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Where Nebraska Stands on Childcare Regulations in 2026
item.description:
Nebraska has a lot to be proud of in the Archbridge Institute’s 2026 State Childcare Regulations Index. Ranking a solid 10th nationwide with a childcare freedom score of 5.48 out of 10, the Cornhusker State stands as a competitive leader in keeping childcare regulations manageable. Within the West North Central region, Nebraska ranks 2nd, demonstrating...

The post Where Nebraska Stands on Childcare Regulations in 2026 appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Mspolicy] Government pays to lobby government — with your tax dollars
item.description:
Across Mississippi, government bodies spend public money hiring lobbyists — to lobby government. I would not have believed quite how much of it goes on until we built the tool that lays it bare. Let me explain how we got here. Within days of entering the White House in January 2025, President Trump set up […]

The post Government pays to lobby government — with your tax dollars appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Sutherlandinstitute] Virtual learning: From online schools to AI tools
item.description:
As Utah policymakers navigate education technology, they should be guided by a simple principle: technology should expand educational opportunity for families while being evaluated honestly for its results on student learning.

The post Virtual learning: From online schools to AI tools appeared first on Sutherland Institute.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] The Debt Crisis is Worse than You Think
item.description:
The national debt has grown from roughly $3 trillion when Ross Perot warned about it in 1992 to more than $39 trillion today, making it one of the largest long-term challenges facing the United States. Decades of deficit spending have pushed debt levels to record highs, and the true burden looks even worse when measured against federal revenues rather than the nation's total economic output. Rising debt and interest costs threaten economic stability, higher taxes, reduced federal benefits, and difficult budget decisions for states like Iowa that depend heavily on federal funding.

The post The Debt Crisis is Worse than You Think appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Empirecenter] Hammond Submits CDPAP Testimony to Congress
item.description:
The Empire Center’s Bill Hammond today submitted written testimony for a congressional hearing on healthcare fraud. Hammond’s testimony to the....

The post Hammond Submits CDPAP Testimony to Congress appeared first on Empire Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Empowerms] Charter Schools Outperform Districts on 3rd Grade Reading Test Initial Results
item.description:
The Mississippi Department of Education (MDE) has released results of the initial administration of the 2026 third-grade reading assessment, and five of the six Mississippi charter schools with third-grade students outperformed their local school district. This assessment process is often referred to as the “Third Grade Gate” and is required under the state’s Literacy-Based Promotion…

The post Charter Schools Outperform Districts on 3rd Grade Reading Test Initial Results first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Forged Signatures, Locked Doors, Stolen Wages: Freedom Foundation Files 36 Charges Against California Unions
item.description:
The Freedom Foundation today announced the filing of 36 unfair labor practice (ULP) charges against Service Employees International Union (SEIU) Local 2015, SEIU Local 721, and United Domestic Workers of America (UDW/AFSCME Local 3930) on behalf of California homecare providers whose wages were taken without their consent.

The post Forged Signatures, Locked Doors, Stolen Wages: Freedom Foundation Files 36 Charges Against California Unions appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] MSPC named national finalist for Marketing Campaign of the Year
item.description: Launched by Mountain States Policy Center following the passage of Idaho's historic education freedom legislation, Idaho Kids Win was designed to ensure families understood and could access the new opportunities available to their children.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Coverage vs. Care (Part 2): What Was Lost and Why
item.description: Before Medicaid, Idahoans who needed medical care and lacked the means to pay for it relied on a number of alternatives: friends and family, charitable care from physicians, church networks, mutual aid societies, and community organizations that knew their neighbors by name. Fraternal orders — the Odd Fellows, the Elks, and the Knights of Columbus […]

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] The Best Way to Eliminate Methane Leaks is to Drill
item.description:
California’s oil industry has been active for well over a century, and Los Angeles was always at the heart of it. By 1894, about 80 wells were already producing oil in the city, setting off a boom that peaked in the 1970s at nearly 100 million barrels per year. Today oil production in Los Angeles County is barely...

The post The Best Way to Eliminate Methane Leaks is to Drill first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Sutherlandinstitute] When city rules chill home worship, courts should step in
item.description:
The specific legal question is whether he can bring his case now based on the threat or whether he will need to wait until the city makes a final determination against him.

The post When city rules chill home worship, courts should step in appeared first on Sutherland Institute.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] We Can’t Afford Metro’s Affordable Housing
item.description:
by Randal O’Toole Affordable housing projects funded with Metro’s 2018 housing bond have...

The post We Can’t Afford Metro’s Affordable Housing appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Washington state’s gas tax is going up in July and will automatically increase yearly; that’s a big problem
item.description: By automatically increasing gas taxes each year, Washington policymakers are ensuring voters will pay even more without the normal public accountability or public debate for a tax increase. This yearly automatic gas tax increase should be towed back to the legislative garage for major repairs.

item.title: [Beacontn] America at 250: Wins Worth Celebration
item.description: The wins we at Beacon celebrate are all grounded in the principles of freedom upon which our nation was founded.

item.title: [Jbartlett] Laconia beats Boston to launch first social district in New England
item.description:
On March 21, Laconia launched New England’s first social district, a designated area where drinking is allowed in public under tightly regulated conditions.  With Motorcycle Week looming, critics on social media mockingly predicted disaster.  A week later, Joia Hughes, Laconia’s economic development and housing director, summed up the damage. “No problems,” she said. Mayor Mike […]

The post Laconia beats Boston to launch first social district in New England appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Lawmakers agree suspended drivers on Wisconsin roads remain a problem
item.description:
While state law prohibits drivers from driving with a suspended license, it’s not a crime unless alcohol is involved.

The post Lawmakers agree suspended drivers on Wisconsin roads remain a problem appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Wisconsin is missing its Medicaid accountability moment
item.description:
At a time when Medicaid fraud is making national news, Wisconsin ought to impose greater discipline on a program that now costs taxpayers $36.4 billion over the 2025-27 budget cycle.

The post Wisconsin is missing its Medicaid accountability moment appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Raising the Standards
item.description:
Texas is on the verge of a major education victory. The State Board of Education (SBOE) is expected to vote tomorrow on adopting improved Texas Essential Knowledge and Skills (TEKS) standards for social studies and the state’s first required literary works list. These proposals address two subjects that Texas students have largely struggled with in...

The post Raising the Standards first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] ‘The more you ask the computer to do for you, the more power you need’
item.description: Dennis Talluto on the fight over data centers

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: Common Sense Catches Up THIRTY YEARS AGO, George Witwer, a founding scholar of the Indiana Policy Review Foundation and now CEO of Cavo Health, won a dark-horse race for the GOP nomination for lieutenant governor at a contested state convention. He did so campaigning on the floor, delegate-by-delegate, with a simple argument, “Property taxes mean you never Read the full article...

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] The Name Game: How Connecticut Teachers Union (AFT) Keeps Dues Spending in the Dark
item.description: Every year, more than 30,000 Connecticut educators’ hand over a portion of their paycheck to the American Federation of Teachers Connecticut. Most never see a detailed accounting of where that money goes. That isn’t an accident, and it […]

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Will Swaim: America’s 250th and the Fight for Liberty in California
item.description:
When I told a friend recently that I was sorry to hear she was getting a divorce, she replied, “You wouldn’t be sorry if you really knew my husband.” When I admitted that I knew him but not really, she followed with a painfully detailed catalog of his offenses and promised that I’d get an...

The post Will Swaim: America’s 250th and the Fight for Liberty in California first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Cities Cannot Budget Their Way Out of Mission Problems
item.description:
California cities continue to struggle with recurring fiscal stress. Los Angeles recently closed a budget gap approaching $1 billion. San Jose addressed a shortfall of roughly $50 million while acknowledging that structural challenges remain. Oakland reopened its two-year budget to address a gap of roughly $265 million while continuing to wrestle with insolvency concerns. Across...

The post Cities Cannot Budget Their Way Out of Mission Problems first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Empirecenter] Healthcare Revelations in the Enacted Budget Financial Plan
item.description:
The state financial plan published on June 10 disclosed key information about healthcare revenue and spending that lawmakers had not....

The post Healthcare Revelations in the Enacted Budget Financial Plan appeared first on Empire Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Empowerms] Amanda Delverdank is building something special in the Mississippi Delta
item.description:
Originally from Utah, Amanda came to Mississippi through Teach for America and began her journey in education as an art teacher in Greenville. Over time, her passion for children, community, and meaningful learning experiences continued to grow. While working in schools, she began noticing something powerful: children often learned best when they were allowed to…

The post Amanda Delverdank is building something special in the Mississippi Delta first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] State Audits Show Why Connecticut Needs ERIN’s Act
item.description: Last week, Yankee Institute proposed the Expenditure Records and Information Notification Act, or ERIN’s Act, a reform requiring executive branch agencies to publish purchasing-card transactions in a centralized, searchable online […]

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Big Loser: Socialism in New York and Texas
item.description:
Even though I’ve taken off from my Winners & Losers newsletter this summer, I still join the Cardle & Woolley Show on Talk 1370 Radio in Austin Friday mornings at 8:30 a.m. to announce the week’s list. You can listen live here every week or download the broadcast anytime by clicking here. This week I...

The post Big Loser: Socialism in New York and Texas first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] New ITR Foundation Poll shows Iowans Want Permanent Protection from Tax Hikes
item.description:
A new Iowans for Tax Relief Foundation (ITRF) poll of likely general election voters in Iowa reveals consistent support for fending off tax hikes, and a political environment that is pointing toward close races up and down the ballot. The survey, conducted from June 16-19, 2026 , found that support for an amendment to Iowa’s constitution requiring a two-thirds supermajority of the state legislature to approve income tax increases has grown to 73.8%, up from 70.5 % last September. This strong backing extends across political lines; 72% of independent voters and 60% of democrat voters favor this protection.

The post New ITR Foundation Poll shows Iowans Want Permanent Protection from Tax Hikes appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] You Don’t Have a Constitutional Right to Free Speech … On Someone Else’s Property
item.description:
The Goldwater Institute this week filed a brief in the U.S. Supreme Court urging it to overrule a decades-old precedent..

The post You Don’t Have a Constitutional Right to Free Speech … On Someone Else’s Property appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Who Decides Who Gets to Be a Lawyer in Louisiana?
item.description:
For more than four decades, a single private trade association in Chicago, the American Bar Association (ABA), has decided who is allowed to become a lawyer in nearly every state. That’s because nearly every state, including Louisiana, relies on the ABA to determine which law schools meet their standards. Almost all states require future lawyers...

The post Who Decides Who Gets to Be a Lawyer in Louisiana? appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF on with Bob Clark for TWO fantastic interviews
item.description:
On Thursday, June 25, 2026 RGF hosted a luncheon with speaker Tim Sandefur as part of the organization’s “America 250” celebrations. Tim signed copies of his book: “Proclaiming Liberty: John Adams, Thomas Jefferson, and the Declaration of Independence.” You can check out the interview Bob Clark of KKOB radio did with Tim below: The following day Paul […]

The post RGF on with Bob Clark for TWO fantastic interviews appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Mackinac] Michigan lawmakers push an affordability agenda
item.description: Bills aim to cut costs imposed by heavy regulation

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Alternative Catholic Education Models Gain Popularity and Attention
item.description:
Recently, I had the pleasure of moderating a panel on alternative Catholic education models at a retreat conference for the Order of Malta, which is a lay order of the Catholic Church founded a millennium ago and which focuses on medical, social, and humanitarian aid projects.  The panel gave insight into the reasons for the […]

The post Alternative Catholic Education Models Gain Popularity and Attention appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Inpolicy] Franke: The Declaration Parchment
item.description: by Mark Franke With the semiquincentennial of the Declaration of Independence, there is a plethora of new books written about this major event. Most focus on the Declaration’s language and its meaning, especially its most memorable sentence in the second paragraph. That’s understandable but I am a contrarian by nature so I have been wondering Read the full article...

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Illinois’ financial transparency ranks among worst in the nation
item.description:
The state ranked 46th in a recent report, getting only 51 out of 100 points.

The post Illinois’ financial transparency ranks among worst in the nation appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Iowa Climbs in National Competitiveness Rankings
item.description:
Iowa's pro-growth tax reforms are delivering results. Iowa climbed from 30th to 25th in the Rich States, Poor States rankings after enacting a 3.8% flat income tax, lowering corporate taxes, and implementing other taxpayer-friendly reforms that have strengthened the state's economy and competitiveness. The work isn't finished. While Iowa has made historic progress, other states are continuing to cut taxes even more aggressively. To remain competitive, Iowa must continue improving its tax climate rather than becoming complacent. Spending restraint is the key to lasting tax relief. Meaningful tax reform is only possible when government spending is controlled. Iowa's new 2% cap on property tax growth builds on that principle and helps lay the foundation for long-term fiscal responsibility.

The post Iowa Climbs in National Competitiveness Rankings appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] The legacy of the Declaration of Independence
item.description: Part of a citizen’s job is to inform the rising generation of what the Declaration really said, what it really means, and why it is important. May we continue that effort long after this 250th anniversary has receded into history.

item.title: [Empirecenter] Fourteen NYC Educators Receive Over Half a Million Dollars in Pensions
item.description:
Fourteen retired New York City educators received more than $500,000 in pension benefits in 2025, according to data released by....

item.title: [Showmeinstitute] The Social Security Crisis Is Worse Than You Think with Andrew G. Biggs
item.description:
Susan Pendergrass speaks with Andrew G. Biggs, senior fellow at the American Enterprise Institute, about the Social Security trustees’ latest report and what it means for the program’s future. They […]

item.description:

item.title: [Mspolicy] AI — Rocket Fuel for the Liberty Revolution
item.description:
Over the years, the liberty movement has won some remarkable victories. Think of how school choice has advanced (albeit not yet in Mississippi), or the wave of flat-tax reforms rolled out across many states. What makes this all the more impressive is that the change has been achieved with often limited resources.  Our opponents are […]

The post AI — Rocket Fuel for the Liberty Revolution appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] New A–F Grades for Kansas Schools Highlight Impact of Lowered Standards
item.description: The newest A–F Grading of Kansas Schools is here. A-F Grading provides Kansans with a clear, easy-to-understand snapshot of academic performance statewide. The A–F system translates complex state assessment data into familiar letter grades, offering a transparent alternative to Kansas’ official school report cards, which often rely on technical labels that are difficult for parents […]

item.title: [Americanexperiment] MN social media warning law scheduled to go into effect July 1 but enforcement currently facing legal challenge
item.description:
Minnesota’s new social media warning law passed during the 2025 special legislative session requires social media companies to display a mental health warning label to users on their sites. The provision is set to go into effect July 1, 2026. But a tech industry group named NetChoice, representing social media and tech companies including Meta,...

The post MN social media warning law scheduled to go into effect July 1 but enforcement currently facing legal challenge appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] Arizona Supreme Court Allows Donor Privacy Challenge to Proceed
item.description:
The Arizona Supreme Court today issued a landmark decision recognizing that the Arizona Constitution provides an independent source of protection..

item.title: [Americanexperiment] St. Paul Public Schools balances budget, accepts teacher cuts
item.description:
Saint Paul Public School District, the second largest district in the state, has just passed a new budget that narrowly avoids any budget shortfalls. It hasn’t always been sunshine and roses lately for the district. Faced with a similar demographic decline as other Minnesota districts, Saint Paul has experienced over a 16.4 percent enrollment decline...

The post St. Paul Public Schools balances budget, accepts teacher cuts appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Why High Energy Prices Will Plague Massachusetts for Years to Come
item.description:
Amid our recent harsh winter, Governor Maura Healey issued an immediate reduction of electricity bills by 25% across the state and a 10% gas bill reduction. However, these are temporary relief measures, as Massachusetts is forced to reconcile how it will address its aging infrastructure, fuel constraints, and the cost pressures placed on consumers.

Currently, Massachusetts’ retail electricity cost ranges from about $0.28-$0.32/kWh, which is about 40-60% higher than the US national average of $0.20/kWh. It is also a sharp increase from Massachusetts’ last recorded rate of $0.23/kWh in 2023, according to Pioneer’s DataLabs, which placed Massachusetts fourth highest nationally. Utilities company Eversource Energy has examined the increase in residential gas bill prices since 2015, breaking down bill components into four categories: maintenance and infrastructure, Gas System Enhancement Plan (GSEP), public benefits that support state-mandated programs (i.e. Mass Save), and supply. While each component has shown increases over the past ten years, public benefits rose 200% and supply related costs rose 175%.

The post Why High Energy Prices Will Plague Massachusetts for Years to Come appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Sutherlandinstitute] Sutherland Institute commends Supreme Court decision affirming states’ constitutional role in elections
item.description:
The Court narrowly and appropriately interpreted federal election law. It upheld the constitutional framework established by Article I, Section 4, which gives states the lead role in elections, as long as they do not conflict with federal law.

The post Sutherland Institute commends Supreme Court decision affirming states’ constitutional role in elections appeared first on Sutherland Institute.

item.description: At Yankee Institute, we envision a Connecticut that is affordable, livable, and workable. Our 2026 Public Policy Toolkit advances that vision by explaining how policy decisions function in practice, making […]

item.description:

item.title: [Pacificresearch] The Real Lesson Of America’s Rising Uninsured Rate
item.description:
The number of uninsured Americans ticked upward to 26.7 million in 2024, according to an analysis published this month by KFF. The report’s authors attribute that trend to the “high cost of private insurance and limited availability of public coverage.” Unpack the numbers, though, and the situation becomes more complicated. The number of uninsured is […]

The post The Real Lesson Of America’s Rising Uninsured Rate appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] George Floyd looter in court on new beef
item.description:
Kevon Devante Rayshun Ousley, 31, appeared in federal court in downtown St. Paul this afternoon, indicted on a felony count of being a felon in possession of a firearm. Your correspondent was there. The previous felony cited in the June 25 federal indictment dated back to a 2016 drive-by shooting incident in north Minneapolis. Ousley...

The post George Floyd looter in court on new beef appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] Matthew Bernard and Lynn Warner v. City of Oakland, California
item.description:
Date Filed: June 29, 2026 Original Court: Northern District of California Case Status: Pending Matthew Bernard and Lynn Warner own an undeveloped residential plot in Oakland, California. In 2020, the Oakland Fire Department ordered them to clear dead and dying trees from their land or face ongoing fines. The landowners hired an arborist and removed...

The post Matthew Bernard and Lynn Warner v. City of Oakland, California first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Soros, son already have pumped $103M into midterms
item.description:
Gabrielle Fahmy, Rich Calder, and David Spector write for the New York Post about a left-wing billionaire’s involvement in this year’s elections. Billionaire Democratic kingmaker George Soros and his son Alex spent a staggering $102.8 million in the midterm election cycle — making the family chief architects of the party’s seismic shift toward the radical left. With the November...

The post Soros, son already have pumped $103M into midterms appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Mamdani believes democratic socialists can win anywhere
item.description:
Julia Cherner writes for ABC News about a disturbing prediction from New York City’s mayor. After the candidates he backed swept their Democratic congressional primaries, New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani said his democratic socialist message can win nationally. “I think a democratic socialist can get elected anywhere across this country for any position,” Mamdani...

The post Mamdani believes democratic socialists can win anywhere appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.description: Will Saginaw Township Community Schools finally listen to voters?

item.title: [Pacificresearch] California’s Bad Energy Policy Is the Real Threat to the AI Boom
item.description:
In early 2026, data centers consumed roughly 1,000 megawatts of electricity, about 2% of California ISO’s peak load. The California Energy Commission projects that figure will reach 4,500 megawatts, or 9% of peak demand, by 2040. That projection sits on top of overall peak demand the CEC expects to rise between 42 and 61% by 2045, driven by […]

The post California’s Bad Energy Policy Is the Real Threat to the AI Boom appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] How a school district cut chronic absenteeism by 65%
item.description:
Nearly 1-in-4 students were chronically absent in 2023 at New Trier High School. That dropped by 65% after reforms were implemented.

item.title: [Johnlocke] What kept North Carolina cool during a recent heat emergency
item.description:
The forecast in North Carolina for June 11–12, 2026, called for extreme heat. A heat advisory was issued for most of the state. WNCN reported that in Raleigh on June 12, the high was “103 degrees with a heat index over 106.” As during last year’s extreme heat wave and earlier this year during a deep freeze, Duke Energy obtained...

The post What kept North Carolina cool during a recent heat emergency appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Inpolicy] Eichenberger: The Linguistic Assault on Reality
item.description: by Dan Eichenberger M.D. Language remains one of humanity’s most powerful tools. Spoken words helped early humans cooperate, hunt and pass down knowledge. Written language allowed civilizations to record laws, ideas and stories for future generations. Today, language is under attack. The political Left, particularly progressive activists who dominate many colleges, news outlets and major corporations, Read the full article...

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] AI in Medicine: More Than a Hypothetical
item.description:
The average American is optimistic about how artificial intelligence (AI) can change healthcare. Anyone who has ever experienced or been near to a difficult health problem understands that the hope that a rapidly developing technology offers is not a small one. And while medical progress is often cited as one of the most valuable and...

The post AI in Medicine: More Than a Hypothetical appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Using AI to Reduce State Regulations
item.description:
Federal regulation has grown into something almost impossible to fully grasp. The U.S. Code of Federal Regulations now runs over 180,000 pages, compared to just 10,000 pages in 1950. States have not fared any better. Ohio's code recently surpassed 17 million words. At that scale, checking every rule against current law to see what is outdated or contradictory is not realistic. Therefore, almost nobody does, and the rules keep piling up.

In the past some states have attempted to arduously tackle the problem by hand. For example, former Massachusetts’ Governor Charlie Baker launched a "regulatory review" effort in 2015 that ran through 2017. Many other states have attempted similar reviews over the years. Reviewing an entire regulatory code, however, is slow and labor-intensive, and many of these earlier efforts ended up incomplete.

The post Using AI to Reduce State Regulations appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Homeowners Insurance Premiums Continues to Rise Amid Climate Risk
item.description:
Across the United States, annual homeowner insurance premiums have increased by an average of $648 from 2021 to 2024, according to the Consumer Federation of America. While there are widespread cost increases nationally, several communities have been hit harder than others due to the rise in severe weather events in the past few years. The states that saw the greatest percentage increase in premiums were Utah (59%), Illinois (50%), Arizona (48%), and Pennsylvania (44%). This contrasts with those states that saw the greatest hike in absolute dollars, which were Florida ($2,118), Louisiana ($1,775), and Kentucky ($1,426).

These two pieces of data tell us two things about the effect of climate risk on insurance premiums: new areas are being exposed to disasters and known high-risk areas are facing exacerbated impacts.

The post Homeowners Insurance Premiums Continues to Rise Amid Climate Risk appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] 250 Years On: What We Owe This Moment
item.description:
Raise a glass to freedom, something they can never take away. My favorite song from Hamilton has been going through my head nonstop all week (my second favorite week of the year, after Mardi Gras!). Every Independence Day, my family does what most Louisiana families do. We cook, we light things on fire, and we...

The post 250 Years On: What We Owe This Moment appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Freedom Foundation named finalist for State Policy Network’s Bob Williams Award
item.description:
The Freedom Foundation’s successful, three-year effort to end taxpayer support for teachers unions in Idaho is receiving national recognition as a strategic, generational win that will save Gem State taxpayers millions of dollars, level the political playing field, and protect teachers from exploitative union practices. Each year, the State Policy Network (SPN) awards state policy […]

The post Freedom Foundation named finalist for State Policy Network’s Bob Williams Award appeared first on Freedom Foundation.
